design_philosophy.core_loop: Platforming action with weapon variety and character-specific mechanics. Players punch/attack enemies, collect bell power-ups for weapon upgrades, charge rocket packs for flight, and navigate level environments. Core tension between managing charge times for different abilities across three character archetypes.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management of charge mechanics - each character has different charge speeds for punch wave vs. rocket propeller, forcing tactical decisions about which ability to prioritize in combat.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as one of three TwinBee pilots with distinct playstyles (TwinBee: balanced, WinBee: fast propeller/slow punch, GwinBee: fast punch/slow propeller), using various weapons (hammer, lasso, throwing rattles, guns) to fight through colorful levels while discovering alternate exits.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Genre departure - first TwinBee platformer instead of vertical shooter. Strong regional game design philosophy: Japanese version features non-linear level interconnection (quadrilateral map with alternate exits like Super Mario World) and narrative integration (Dr. Warumon/Dr. Cinnamon dialogue), while European version forces strict linear progression and removes story elements. This suggests intentional design trade-offs between exploration freedom and accessibility.

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Character selection affects difficulty through charge time mechanics - WinBee offers faster propeller (evasion advantage) but slow punch, GwinBee offers fast punch but slow propeller. Regional differences also affect difficulty: Japanese version's non-linear design and alternate exits allow players to choose stage order and skip content; European version forces linear progression with no escape routes.

progression.upgrade_system: Bell power-up system with color-based effects. Bells obtained by defeating enemies (not shooting clouds like other TwinBee games). Power-ups include weapon sets, guns, speed boosts, options, and invincibility frames.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Japanese version: Quadrilateral stage map with interconnected levels and alternate exits per stage (similar to Super Mario World design). Some stages are gated behind clearing others, but players have choice in order. European version: Strictly linear - specific level cannot be accessed without clearing all previous stages. Battery save backup (Japanese) or password system (both versions, European exclusive).
replayability_hooks: Three playable characters with distinct charge-time mechanics creating different strategies
Alternate stage exits and non-linear level progression (Japanese version only)
Multiple endings based on stage completion percentage (Japanese version only)
Versus mode for multiplayer competition across three rounds
Bell power-up color variety encourages experimentation

world_building_techniques: Character-specific pilots with personality (Light, Pastel, Mint, TwinBee, WinBee, GwinBee)
Level select screen dialogue between Dr. Cinnamon, Dr. Warumon, and pilots (Japanese version only - removed in European version)
Series continuity nods: gun mechanics reference Detana!! TwinBee cutscene, bell power-up system references main series
Contextual narrative framing through character interactions rather than heavy exposition

faction_indicators: Three pilot characters (Light, Pastel, Mint) with distinct identities serve different player preferences
danger_signals: Charge mechanic complexity and character asymmetry could create skill expression differentiation among players
environmental_storytelling: Level design choices reflect character abilities - stages may subtly favor certain pilots' charge mechanics
Japanese version's branching design allows players to express preference through stage selection
Bell power-up placement and enemy distribution could reinforce character strengths/weaknesses
